memories.text: In June 1766 Rousseau wrote a violent letter to Hume, and in spring 1767 he fled to Spalding, then to Calais, landing in May.
Rousseau lived with Therese in Wootton and devoted his time to herborising and inditing the first six books of his Confessions.
Rousseau left the Isle de Saint-Pierre, travelled to Strasbourg, then to Paris arriving December 16, 1765, where Prince de Conti provided lodging at Hotel Saint-Simon.

In London, Rousseau was offered a home at Wootton, Staffordshire by Mr. Davenport for a rent of 30 L per year, and he was accorded a pension of 100 L by George III but declined after the first payment.
Rousseau's works 'Reveries d'un Promeneur Solitaire' and 'La Nouvelle Heloise' are closely allied in tone and spirit to the Confessions.
Rousseau had a profound sense of justice and a real desire for the improvement and advancement of the race.
Rousseau had a mistress named Madame de Warens, whom he called his 'Mamma'.
Rousseau renounced his father's duty but later expiated it through a long and bitter repentance.

The Confessions covers leading personages and transactions of a momentous epoch when absolutism and feudalism were rallying against the modern spirit represented by Voltaire, the Encyclopedists, and Rousseau himself.
Lord Byron's writings influenced Rousseau, and Byron expressed sympathy with Rousseau's character in some stanzas of 'Childe Harold'.
Rousseau suffered from an incurable disease for most of his life.
The book 'The Confessions of Jean Jacques Rousseau' was privately printed for members of the Aldus Society in London in 1903 by David Widger.
